jokes.text: CloudTrail is the office snitch. Every meeting, every coffee break, every bathroom trip — logged. The boss loves him. Everyone else avoids him.
EC2 is your flatmate who leaves the lights on. Forgot about him for 3 weeks? Bill says hi. $147. He's still 'running.'
S3 Glacier is the storage unit your auntie put your childhood toys in. You can get them back, but you'll wait 12 hours and pay for the truck.
The root user is your house keys taped to the front door. AWS literally begs you to lock them in a drawer. Some people don't listen. Some people get hacked.
AWS Pricing Calculator: where you go to confirm what you've already spent, and gasp.
Auto Scaling is the trotro driver who watches the road. Five passengers? One trotro. A hundred passengers? Suddenly there are ten trotros. Magic.
Route 53 is the GPS that's been giving directions since 2010. Why 53? Because it's port 53. Why port 53? DNS, child. DNS.
DynamoDB is the librarian who insists you tell her which shelf BEFORE looking at the book. Doesn't matter how big the library gets — she's instant.
RDS is the responsible older sibling of databases. Backs herself up, patches herself, restarts herself. The only one Mum trusts to run the household.
CloudFront is the WhatsApp forward of the internet. Your video in Accra? Already in Tokyo, London, and Lagos. Faster than gossip from Auntie.
SNS shouts in the village square. Everyone subscribed hears it. SQS quietly slips notes under each person's door. Same news, different vibes.
VPC is the wall around your compound. Subnets are the rooms. Security groups are the housekeeper deciding who enters. NACLs are the dogs at the gate.
Bedrock is the AI buffet. Claude, Llama, Titan, Mistral — they're all there. AWS doesn't cook; they just charge you for plates.
Free Tier is like a free first lesson at gym. By month 4, you owe $40 and don't remember signing up. Set billing alarms, friend.
CloudFormation is the architect who writes 800-line letters describing a 1-bedroom apartment. Stack failed? Read the letter again. Slowly.
S3 never forgets. You uploaded a cringe selfie in 2014? Still there. Still costs you 0.023 cents a month. S3 is your eternal hard drive of regret.
Lambda is that friend who shows up exactly when needed, does the job, then disappears. Never asks for money. Pays for himself by the millisecond.
IAM policies are 800 lines of JSON that say one of three things: 'Allow', 'Deny', or 'You'll find out at 3 AM in production.'
Reserved Instances are AWS's way of saying: 'Marry us for 3 years and we'll give you a discount. You will not break up. We have your card.'
Reading AWS docs is like reading a recipe in 14 languages, with footnotes that link to other recipes, that link to MORE footnotes. By page 11 you've forgotten what you wanted to cook.
Beanstalk is for developers who saw EC2, VPC, and Load Balancers and said 'I will simply… not.' AWS heard them and built a magic button.
CloudWatch sees everything. Logs everything. Charges you for everything. Then asks if you'd like to enable enhanced monitoring for an extra fee.
ECS is AWS saying 'we'll handle the containers.' EKS is AWS saying 'we'll handle the Kubernetes that handles the containers, but you still suffer.'
Aurora is what happens when MySQL goes to a luxury spa, gets a makeover, and comes back charging 3x the price. Worth it? Sometimes. Painful? Always.
An us-east-1 outage is the AWS equivalent of ECG power going off in Accra. Half the world stops working. People panic. Twitter wakes up. Then it's fine. Until next time.
Step Functions: when 'just chain a few Lambdas' becomes a 200-state visual workflow that even YOU don't understand anymore.
The AWS Console has 250 services on the homepage. You came to launch ONE EC2 instance. 45 minutes later, you're reading about quantum computing. Focus.
Opening Cost Explorer feels like opening your bank app after Detty December. You knew it would hurt. You just didn't know it would hurt THIS much.
Step 1: pass Cloud Practitioner. Step 2: feel like a cloud expert. Step 3: open the Solutions Architect Associate exam. Step 4: humility.
Spot Instances are AWS letting you bid for leftover compute. Cheap as anything. Until AWS takes it back with 2 minutes notice. Then you cry.
KMS encrypts your data so well that even YOU can't read it. Lose the key? Congratulations — you've invented data destruction-as-a-service.
Tagging your resources is like labelling your kenkey containers in a shared fridge. Skip it once, and three months later nobody knows which one is yours.
Lambda cold starts: that awkward 800ms where your function is yawning, stretching, brushing its teeth, before it can actually do the job.
us-east-1 is where AWS itself runs. When it sneezes, the internet catches a cold. Half the services say 'Global region' but secretly mean 'us-east-1 or nothing.'
Basic Support: 'Read the docs.' Developer: 'Email us during business hours.' Business: 'We'll answer in 4 hours.' Enterprise: 'Here's our cell number, sir, what's wrong?'
MFA is your phone going 'are you really you?' every five minutes. Annoying. Until the day a hacker gets your password and your phone saves your job.
Untagged AWS resources are like that uncle nobody remembers inviting to the wedding. He's there. He's eating. Who brought him? Who pays his bill?
'Serverless' means there are servers, you just don't see them. Like 'cloud' means computers, you just don't see them. AWS marketing 101: out of sight, in your wallet.
Auto Scaling DOWN is the trotro mate at noon. Empty bus. He looks at the others, sighs, and goes home to sleep. 'I'll come back at 5,' he says. He keeps his word.
AWS Always Free Tier is real. It's also 5 GB of S3, 1 million Lambda requests, and 750 hours of t2.micro EC2. Generous? Yes. Enough for production? Don't make me laugh.
